The price of TAO surged from $243 before the podcast to $365, a monthly increase of over 100%. Open interest in futures contracts ballooned to $639 million, compared to less than $132 million at the beginning of March. Grayscale had already submitted its S-1 application for a TAO spot ETF to the SEC at the end of December 2025, with Bitwise following suit on the same day. But what truly unsettled those in the industry wasn't the price, but something that happened on March 10th. Bittensor's subnet 3 (Templar) announced that it had trained a 72 billion-parameter language model using over 70 ordinary computers scattered around the world, connected via an average 500 Mb/s home broadband connection. This model, called Covenant-72B, achieved a score of 67.1 on the MMLU benchmark—approaching or even surpassing Meta's LLaMA-2-70B, trained using a cluster of supercomputers. A group of individual users' graphics cards trained a large-scale model that rivals Silicon Valley giants. Even more bizarrely, no one knows where these nodes are located. They might be in a rented apartment in Tokyo, in a hacker's basement in Berlin, or even in your next-door neighbor's house. All contribution records and reward distributions are written on the Bittensor blockchain—transparent, immutable, and permissionless. This isn't just a story of "cheap computing power." It's a war about "who has the right to train AI." What is it doing—128 smart factories operating simultaneously? The success of Covenant-72B isn't based on brute force, but on an algorithm called SparseLoCo. In short, this algorithm compresses the training data that needs to be transmitted between nodes by 146 times, achieving a compression rate of over 97%, while maintaining almost no loss of model accuracy. This means you no longer need a multi-billion dollar supercomputing center like Nvidia's; a 500 Mbps home broadband connection is sufficient. But Bittensor does much more than just train a single model. It currently has 128 concurrently running subnets, each performing a different task. You can think of the entire network as a giant industrial park: Factory 3 (Templar) is responsible for training large models; Factory 64 (Chutes) provides inference services, reportedly with over 400,000 users and processing 5 million requests daily, at only 15% of AWS's price; Factory 4 (Targon) handles confidential GPU computing, and Intel engineers even co-authored a technical white paper on trusted execution environments with it—not just a business collaboration with a logo, but two Intel engineers who actually put their names on it; Factory 51 (Lium) is the GPU P2P market, reportedly managing a cluster of over 500 H100 computing power. The head effect is very obvious—the top ten subnets take 56% of the network's emissions rewards. But the economic mechanism itself is even more noteworthy. After November 2025, Bittensor launched the Taoflow model: the amount of TAO rewards each subnet receives is no longer determined by a vote of 64 validators, but directly by the flow of market funds. Rewards go to the subnets where funds flow. An 86.8-day exponential moving average automatically adjusts, and subnets with continuous net outflows will be "starved." Even more ingenious is the dTAO mechanism—each subnet now has its own Alpha token. Investors stake TAO to obtain Alpha from specific subnets, essentially betting on a specific vertical direction. According to v1 research data, as of March, the total market capitalization of all subnet Alpha tokens was approximately $1.12 billion, accounting for about 27% of TAO's own market capitalization. In simpler terms: Bittensor is not an AI product, but a smart production protocol that allows idle GPUs worldwide to automatically team up, compete, and be eliminated. Why it matters—not "cheap," but "sovereignty." On the surface, Bittensor's selling point is that "decentralized computing power is cheaper than centralized power." But this claim doesn't hold up to scrutiny—we'll explain why later. The core of what Jensen Huang said in his podcast wasn't actually price. He said the future of AI isn't about OpenAI dominating, but rather "A and B coexisting": proprietary models and open-source/distributed models will run in parallel for a long time. The reason is simple—fields like healthcare, defense, and manufacturing require complete control over AI models, and this control can only come from open-source or distributed architectures. At the GTC conference, Jensen Huang proposed the concept of a "token factory"—in the future, not only text will be tokenized, but protein structures, robot movements, and physical simulations will also be tokenized. Every scientific field will need its own "intelligence generator." Bittensor's 128 subnets are precisely the prototype of this vision. But a deeper reason is: what if US regulations suddenly tighten, what if a country blocks OpenAI's API, what if your industry is prohibited from using certain closed-source models—what will you do? Bittensor provides an AI infrastructure that is "censorship-resistant." Model weights are open source, nodes are globally distributed, and no single point can be shut down. When Intel engineers chose to co-publish the white paper with Targon, and when early Uber investor Jason Calacanis predicted TAO's 200x growth potential and specifically established the Stillcore Capital fund to bet on TAO and its subnet tokens, they weren't attracted by its "cheapness." They were attracted by the fact that this might be the only AI network that can still function under extreme circumstances. What Bittensor is really selling isn't computing power, it's sovereignty. Doesn't anyone see a problem with this? Now let's look at the other side of the coin. TAO currently has a market capitalization of approximately $3.9 billion. But its most successful subnet, Chutes, only generates $1.3 million to $2.4 million in annualized external revenue. Meanwhile, Chutes receives $52 million worth of TAO subsidies annually from the protocol. Let's calculate the subsidy ratio: 22:1 to 40:1. For every $1 a user pays, the network issues $22 to $40 worth of TAO through inflation to subsidize miners. If these subsidies were removed, Chutes' claim of "15% of the AWS price" would instantly reverse to "1.6 to 3.5 times the AWS price." The network's externally confirmed annual revenue is between $3 million and $15 million, corresponding to a revenue multiple of 175 to 400 times. This figure for traditional high-growth SaaS companies typically doesn't exceed 50 times. So why is the market buying in? Three things: First, TAO's total supply cap of 21 million tokens and its halving mechanism make it as scarce as Bitcoin; second, the expectation of a Grayscale ETF, which, if approved, will open up channels for institutional funds; and third, the narrative itself of being "the only decentralized AI protocol capable of running large models." On-chain data also tells a bullish story. Exchanges saw a net outflow of $5.77 million on March 21-22—large investors were accumulating tokens. According to OnchainLens monitoring, the associated address of well-known trader jez established a 5x leveraged long position in TAO on March 25, with a notional value exceeding $2.7 million. The MACD turned positive in March, and short sellers are being squeezed. However, before the second halving at the end of 2026, if the external revenue of subnets does not increase significantly, the exit of miners could lead to a substantial reduction in hashrate. Bittensor's high beta also means that if the Federal Reserve continues its hawkish stance or the situation in the Middle East spirals out of control, institutional funds will withdraw from these overvalued assets immediately. In summary, Bittensor is either building a decentralized AI "intelligent internet" or constructing a beautiful castle in the air using inflation. The difference between these two outcomes may only be a halving cycle. Jensen Huang said that future factories will produce tokens. But factories will also go bankrupt—especially those factories that can't sell their products and rely entirely on subsidies. Of TAO's $365, how much is the price of intelligence, and how much is the premium for faith? Nobody knows for sure right now.

data.title: The most important thing in Web3 primary market investment
data.description:
If Web3 from 2017 to 2021 was a blue ocean, then investing in the Web3 primary market from the end of 2021 to the end of 2025 can be described as a minefield. The investment logic and environment have shifted from simply being "driven by the project's future narrative" to "economic models, further scrutiny, and a dual drive of data and logic." The difficulty level has increased significantly, especially for primary capital. The CLiff policy, with its unlocking cycle similar to Web2, has greatly affected the activity of new projects. Among the 70 new projects listed on the largest cryptocurrency exchange in 2025, 95% showed a consistent downward trend. What caused this? Based on the current market environment, we will elaborate on several key judgment factors for early-stage rounds in this section.

1. The Essence of the Project: The Value Changes and Judgments of Entrepreneurs

In the Web3 field, the team is the only non-standardized variable and the soul of early-stage projects. Judging entrepreneurs requires not only looking at their impressive resumes but also penetrating their "crypto-native" level, the authenticity of their technical deliverables, and their moral bottom line in the face of extreme interests.

Since many projects in the primary market are still in their nascent stages, the capabilities, vision, and professional ethics of the entrepreneurial team are crucial. Having a keen eye for talent has become one of the most important talents and abilities for investors in this sector. GitHub commit frequency: Don't just listen to the technical vision in the pitch deck. In the AI ​​era, many business plans are even generated by AI with a single click, filled with AI fantasies. For technology-driven projects (such as Infra, ZK, L2), it is essential to have a technical partner or other technical institution review the underlying code on GitHub. However, since many early-stage projects haven't even started building their products yet, this step is highly dependent on the entrepreneur's past abilities and reputation. For example, with Algorand's technological vision at the time, knowing that an MIT Turing Award winner was at the helm of the project's underlying technology naturally lowered the difficulty of your technical due diligence. But by 2026, as the number of established, high-quality projects that could easily attract newcomers decreased, and the number of young entrepreneurs starting new projects increased, the requirements for judging people and conducting technical due diligence reached a new level. **Activity Trap:** Be wary of projects that suddenly commit code before securing funding but remain inactive for extended periods afterward. Data from 2025 shows that projects without audits or with chronically inactive codebases have an 80% higher risk of rg pulls. **Contributor Diversity:** Check whether the code was submitted by a single person or has diverse contributors. Large projects maintained by a single person often imply a very high "bus factor" risk. **While there are more OPC companies in the AI ​​era, the oversight and security risk screening by senior technical personnel for large projects remains crucial.** **Risks of Hiring Executives with Web2 Experience:** Be wary of teams with "parachuted" executives who have impeccable resumes at major Web2 companies but no prior experience in the Web3 blockchain. These types of hires can easily bring bad management practices from Web2 to the Web3 ecosystem, leading to incompatibility and hindering the project's later operation. Unless other members of the team have extensive Web3 experience to mitigate the risks, the risk is too high in the 2025-2026 period. Web3's decentralized governance and community culture are vastly different from Web2, and these hires often lack practical experience in handling community crises (such as governance attacks, fork threats, and economic models). Serial Entrepreneurs: In-depth Attribution of Past Failures: Serial entrepreneurs enjoy a premium in Web3, but it's crucial to distinguish between "honorable failures," "malicious exploitation," and "pure novices." Soft Rug Detection: Investigate whether the previous project experienced a soft rug, i.e., the team stopped development after fundraising citing "poor market conditions" without returning the funds. Treasury Misuse History: Examine the multisignature wallet records of previous projects to confirm whether there was any misappropriation of public funds for personal investment or high-risk financial management without community voting.

2. Judging the resources behind the project

Around 2026, the primary market of the Web3 industry has also entered a new stage. There are some confusing points. In the past year, major exchanges have frequently listed meme and sentiment-based coins, resulting in many projects with underlying technology and less emphasis on marketing not being favored.

2. Judging the resources behind the project

Around 2026, the primary market of the Web3 industry has also entered a new stage. There are some confusing points. In the past year, major exchanges have frequently listed meme and sentiment-based coins, which has led to many projects with underlying technology and less emphasis on marketing not being favored.

2. Judging the resources behind the project

2026, the primary market of the Web3 industry has also entered a new stage. There are some confusing points. In the past year, major exchanges have frequently listed meme and sentiment-based coins, resulting in many projects with underlying technology and less emphasis on marketing not being favored.

2. Judging the resources behind the project

2026, the primary market of the Web3 industry has entered a new stage.

 This situation may gradually improve (Supplement: As of the end of March 2026, Binance has taken the lead in optimizing and improving the quality of listed projects, reducing pure meme-based projects; however, the lingering effects of the past two years still need time to be eliminated. The market is no longer interested in the valuation of new projects, and the listing effect is incomparable to that around 2020. If Binance is like this, the aftereffects on other major exchanges will be even more obvious). However, for new projects, it is still necessary to assess the resources behind them. The core of resource assessment lies in distinguishing between "value-added capital" and "scythe-like capital," the level of advisors, and whether there are acquaintances behind the scenes helping to operate the project. To truly assess the value of a partner, it's crucial to consider not only who invested but also how they invested and what they did afterward. While investments from a16z, Paradigm, Polychain, and Coinbase are strong signals, it's essential to distinguish between lead and follow investments. Furthermore, due to the aforementioned Cliff crisis, the actual returns of top VCs have plummeted. Many VCs within the industry have faced bankruptcy or been forced to launch larger rounds of financing. Moreover, much of what many VCs invested in over the past four years is likely underwater. Therefore, follow-up investments from top VCs may be more of a ripple effect, and the true value judgment ultimately rests with the individual investor.

  • Co-investment Judgment: If a top-tier VC only co-invests with a very small share (e.g., $30k - $100k) and does not join the board of directors (PS: However, it is also necessary to pay attention to the round of investment and valuation, If they enter at a very low valuation, such as 3M, then even tens of thousands of USDT is a very high percentage), this is often a quota given by the project team to "gild" their image, or it is purely a favor. Generally, investment institutions cannot obtain such a cheap valuation. If a VC only participates in a small portion of subsequent high-valuation investments, then this type of "logo investment" does not necessarily mean that the VC will provide actual resource support (such as recruitment, legal assistance, or token model design). However, for some particularly sought-after projects, a small share of undervalued tokens might provide strong endorsement; this needs to be judged on a case-by-case basis. The value of public blockchain foundation grants: Compared to VC investments, grants from major public blockchains such as the Ethereum Foundation, Solana Foundation, or NEAR and BSC Foundations usually represent relatively more robust technical recognition. The due diligence process for these grants often focuses more on code implementation than that for VCs. However, this is only relative. As the industry heats up and applications increase, along with the addition and frequent reshuffling of new Web2 personnel, the judgment levels of foundation staff vary considerably. The same brand can see a drastically different level of performance simply due to a change in the lead. For example, Binance Labs (now renamed YZI Labs) and Algorand both experienced investment quality issues due to large-scale personnel changes. However, Binance, with its strong cash flow from its exchange business, has a level of experimentation and tolerance that most VCs lack. The "fast track" of exchange relationships: Assessing whether early investors have direct connections to Binance, Coinbase, Upbit, etc. Institutions with strong ties to exchanges often receive higher trust and review priority. These resources can significantly increase the probability of a project being listed on a Tier 1 exchange, which is key to solving exit liquidity issues. Identifying Scythe-Shaped Capital and Advisors: Thoroughly research VC brands and individuals whose reputations have fluctuated in the past. While there are no overtly insider listings in the Web3 industry, there are still individual cases where listings are achieved due to personal connections. In such cases, it's crucial to thoroughly investigate whether the projects involved have a poor reputation or involve institutions or market participants who have repeatedly exploited investors, and these should be avoided as early as possible. (PS: However, due to the emergence of Cliff, there are actually fewer VC-level projects. Instead, there are often less responsible project teams offering ecosystem-wide tokens and airdropped, front-running tokens – these are less constrained and more covert. This has led to both investors and VCs being exploited by projects, causing a sustained negative impact on new project launches in the industry.) 3. Judging the Popularity of a Relevant Sector Investment timing and sector selection are crucial in determining the upper limit of return on investment (ROI). This dimension of judgment cannot rely solely on market sentiment; it requires a comprehensive approach, utilizing Gartner curve positioning, on-chain data verification, and macro-geopolitical arbitrage strategies to identify genuine value opportunities amidst the noise. The essence of investing is the monetization of knowledge, and the level of knowledge depends on the judgment of cycles. Recovery-stage sectors (steady growth): Sectors such as DeFi lending and RWA (Real-World Assets) have passed the FOMO bubble burst and entered the "enlightenment and ascent phase." These sectors are characterized by generating real income (Real Yield), having clear business models, and possessing a professional and loyal user base. At this time, primary market investment strategies, including some secondary market investments, should focus on finding undervalued leading companies, or middleware with a large user base that hasn't been adequately promoted in the Web3 space, or those dedicated to solving specific efficiency problems, primarily pursuing high-certainty Beta returns. Sectors in the "expectation inflation period" (high-risk speculative): AI x Crypto (decentralized computing power/agent) and DePIN (decentralized physical infrastructure) are typically at the peak of their "expectation inflation period" in 2024-2025. Current valuations generally include a huge "narrative premium," leading to severe over-investment. Investing in such sectors requires extremely strong "quick in, quick out" capabilities, aiming for high-explosive alpha returns. However, due to the current clipping cycle, it's more advisable to participate in projects with long-term leadership potential that can withstand the test of time and market cycles. Simultaneously, one must be constantly wary of the risk of a narrative bubble bursting. Distinguishing between technological moats and narrative moats: In judging popularity, it's necessary to differentiate whether a project builds its barriers through core technology (such as the novel proof mechanism of ZK-Rollup) or maintains its popularity through marketing rhetoric (such as "intent-driven architecture"). The former still has room to survive after the hype fades, while the latter is often short-lived. Deep Penetration of On-Chain Data and Narrative Falsification: Social media noise is often a contrarian indicator; only on-chain data doesn't lie. Of course, there can also be false, short-term data that manipulates public opinion. Structural Differences Between Genuine and False Popularity: When using Dune Analytics or Nansen to track fund flows, one should not only look at the absolute value of TVL, but also at its composition and retention. Structural popularity manifests as follows: when token incentives cease or the market declines, TVL and the number of active wallets continue to grow or remain stable (e.g., the Layer 2 sector in 2024-2025, driven by genuine demand within the Ethereum ecosystem). Identifying characteristics of false prosperity: if data fluctuates wildly with coin prices, or is primarily supported by a few whale addresses, it is likely a false prosperity built on marketing campaigns (such as "vampire attack" mining). Key areas to focus on: **Cohort Analysis:** What is the retention rate of the first batch of users who joined due to the airdrop after 3 months? If it's below 5%, it indicates a lack of real product-market fit. **Smart Money Flow Monitoring:** Track wallet addresses tagged as "Smart Money" or belonging to well-known institutions. If they are quietly accumulating positions in a particular sector, this is often a leading indicator of an impending sector boom, more effective than any research report. Regulatory Arbitrage and Timing of the Macro Liquidity Cycle: Web3 is a global market, where geopolitics and macroeconomics profoundly influence the ceiling of its growth potential. Regulatory Sensitivity and Geopolitical Arbitrage: Different sectors exhibit vastly different sensitivities to regulation. Privacy Pools and Mixers faced intense global anti-money laundering (AML) scrutiny in 2025, posing extremely high compliance risks and requiring extreme caution in investment. However, some regulatory policies at the end of 2025 ironically stimulated additional interest and market activity in certain privacy-related sectors. Meanwhile, the RWA (Responsiveness, Money Laundering) sector benefited from the compliance framework established by traditional financial institutions, enjoying a "compliance premium." Furthermore, it's crucial to pay attention to policy benefits in different regions (such as policy changes in Hong Kong and mainland China, the US, the EU, Dubai, and Singapore). Investing in projects that align with local policy guidelines can provide policy protection. Precise Positioning in the Macro Liquidity Cycle: Primary market investment is cyclical, requiring prediction of the macroeconomic environment 2-3 years in advance. Avoid heavy investment during the "distribution period" (peak of a bull market, the eve of an interest rate hike cycle), as primary market valuations are severely overextended at this time, with a high probability of immediate price drops upon listing. The optimal entry point is often at the end of the "accumulation period" (early stages of an interest rate cut cycle or early stages of QE projections). At this time, market noise is minimal, valuations return to rationality, and projects have sufficient time to refine their products in a low-cost environment, patiently awaiting the opportune moment. (PS: VCs who invested heavily in 2021 should understand this point best.) 4. Judging by Successful Investment or Large-Scale Project Operation Experience Web3 projects have an extremely high mortality rate. The founding team's "survival skills"—namely, operational capabilities, crisis management experience, and understanding of the unique features of Web3—are crucial to determining the project's lifespan. The market situation in 2026 has changed dramatically. Between 2013 and 2019, many were simply exploring the blockchain industry, and there were countless inexperienced professionals switching careers. However, by 2026, the market will have matured significantly, and the pace and speed of change in trending topics will be extremely rapid. If entrepreneurs lack experience in Web3 startups, investments, or hands-on operations, the difficulty and probability of project failure will increase dramatically. The "second-time success rate premium" for serial entrepreneurs shows that the success rate of founders starting a second business is significantly higher than that of first-time entrepreneurs (approximately 18% vs. less than 10%). Teams that have weathered a complete bull-bear cycle (4-year cycle) have a stronger grasp of cyclical risks and financial management. If the founders have prior experience in successfully listing and operating projects, and their previous projects haven't resulted in fraudulent activities or collapses, then their understanding of compliance structures, listing processes, and market capitalization management, along with their industry reputation, will be significant intangible assets, enabling them to avoid 90% of compliance and financial pitfalls. Operational Metrics and Go-To-Market (GTM) Execution: Assess whether the team possesses "cold start" capabilities, such as acquiring real users through well-designed growth strategies (like Galxe tasks), and having convenient and quick networks and KOL relationships, rather than simply attracting "bargain hunters." Meanwhile, experienced teams will implement diversified reserve management, holding stablecoins (USDC/USDT) sufficient for 12-24 months of operation to ensure sufficient runway for survival during extreme market conditions and bear markets. Community Governance and Crisis Management: If team members are experienced, it's important to examine how the team handled price crashes in past projects. Did they choose transparent compensation and proactive communication (post-mortem), or did they simply close the comment section and remain silent? Transparency is the cornerstone of community trust and a safeguard for a project's survival in bear markets. 5. Judgment of Valuation Reasonableness and Principles While anonymity is part of the crypto spirit, in the current institutional-dominated primary market, fully anonymous teams are often seen as a red flag, increasing the risk of them running away with investors' money. Discount Logic: Projects from anonymous teams should typically be valued 20%-40% lower than those from verified teams to compensate for trust risks. Compliance Requirements: If the team insists on remaining anonymous to the public, they must undergo private KYC (Know Your Customer) verification through a trusted third-party auditing firm (such as CertiK or SlowMist) to ensure legal recourse in case of malicious absconding. Valuations of projects in different sectors can fluctuate significantly. In a bull market, projects in hot sectors and highly sought-after by many will quickly sell out their quotas, often requiring a strong network to secure them in advance. However, some projects, upon closer inspection, possess exceptional potential, but some institutions and individuals may not have recognized this in the short term. These are generally undervalued gems in the primary market, heavily reliant on investors' grasp of long-term value and intuition. 6. Judging the Quality of Unlocking Periods and Release Models Token economics is not only about incentives, but also directly related to investors' exit returns. Poor models can cause the coin price to fall into a "death spiral" or experience prolonged negative selling pressure. Cliff Lock-up Period vs. Linear Vesting: This is a lock-up period commonly seen in projects after the 2020 bull market, typically consisting of a 1-2 year Cliff (lock-up period) followed by a 3-4 year linear vesting. Linear vesting (monthly/block-based) is significantly better than large quarterly or annual vesting, as the latter can cause periodic panic selling. However, this depends on the specific situation, as a fixed-cycle vesting also means there is little selling pressure before the vesting. The most dangerous type is daily vesting, which creates daily selling pressure anxiety for secondary investors, affecting their interest in directly participating in the project, and also incentivizing nodes and private investors to sell tokens daily. A classic example is Algorand's early daily linear unlocking model, which led to prolonged selling pressure, so much so that the project team later changed to an incentive-based unlocking model to alleviate the situation. (Although I really like Algorand and have fond memories of the industry, I avoid daily unlocking models altogether.) If the team's or early investors' cleiff is shorter than 6 months, or the TGE unlocking ratio is too high (>20%), it creates a huge "supply wall" risk. However, I personally believe that the huge early TGE selling pressure might not be a bad thing for particularly high-quality projects, as it provides secondary investors who didn't have the opportunity to participate in the private placement stage with a relatively low-cost entry opportunity, and the subsequent selling pressure is also reduced. (The most obvious example is Solana's private placement, which released its listing mechanism, essentially giving VCs 100% of their TGE. Many VCs then dumped their tokens, giving secondary investors the opportunity to enter at $1. Solana subsequently reached a high of around $300.) Inflation Rate and Distribution Structure: Inflation Stress Testing is Crucial: If a project primarily attracts users through high emissions and lacks a revenue burning mechanism, the token will go to zero due to hyperinflation. Regarding distribution ratios, the community/project pool should hold >50% to ensure long-term incentives; investors and the team typically each hold 15%-20%. If investors make up too much of the investment (>30-40%), the project is prone to becoming a "VC token" and being boycotted by the community. However, all release ratios need to be adjusted according to specific circumstances. There is no perfect model, only responsible project teams and the most suitable model. (PS: In 2026, it was rare for projects to be boycotted because of VC tokens; most of the criticism came from bad or irresponsible project teams, as evidenced by the recent Backpack incident.) The most crucial factor is the team's own ethical standards: Few new projects launched during the 2022-2026 cycle have shown particularly good trends, and a key reason is... Although the VC's tokens were clamped down, the opening price saw a very high FDV (Front-Term Value), incentivizing project teams to prematurely dump their ecosystem share tokens or airdropped "insider trading" tokens into the market, leading to a situation where investors were exploited. Almost all new projects in recent years have faced this problem. In many new projects, aside from the project team and exchanges (through transaction fees), almost no one profits, which undoubtedly creates a vicious cycle and is detrimental to the healthy development of the industry. In summary, the Web3 primary market in 2026 has long since moved beyond the era of blindly investing and exploiting narrative dividends. The current market environment is more like a ruthless "revealing mirror": extremely high FDV, complex Cliff unlocking dilemmas, and hidden insider trading have transformed investment logic from simply "looking at the sector and the institutions" to a deep "game of human nature and economic models." In this hellishly challenging period, the logos of big-name institutions are no longer a get-out-of-jail-free card, and the hype of narratives often hides dangers. For investors, only by returning to common sense—rigorously examining the founding team's ethical bottom line, dissecting the supply and demand dynamics of token releases, and penetrating the authenticity of on-chain data—can they filter out truly valuable targets from this minefield. There is no perfect model, only builders who respect the market and take responsibility for it.

data.title: Two Weeks of Observations on AI in China: Hardware Shock, Software Pessimism, Founder Surprise
data.description:
Author: José Maria Macedo, Co-founder of Delphi Labs; Translated by: Jinse Finance Claw

>

I spent two weeks in China meeting with founders, venture capitalists (VCs), and CEOs of publicly traded companies across the entire AI ecosystem. Before going, I was optimistic about this ecosystem, expecting to find world-class AI geniuses starting businesses at a fraction of Western valuations.

When I left, my perspective had become more refined: I was more confident in hardware than expected, more pessimistic about software, and some of the views of Chinese founders surprised me.

Founder Issues

The great founders I've invested in all share highly recognizable traits: independent thinking, rebelliousness, extreme focus, and near-obsessiveness. They don't take orders, constantly ask "why," and refuse to accept ready-made conventions.

Their decisions are incomprehensible to outsiders, but perfectly natural to themselves. They possess an inner, unyielding intensity, often manifested in a long-term obsession with excellence. Their lives have a certain "edginess," making them instantly recognizable among the countless highly intelligent individuals encountered by VCs. But many of the Chinese founders I've met belong to a different archetype—which surprised me. They are exceptionally talented—graduates of prestigious universities, former employees of ByteDance or DJI, published papers in *Nature*, and hold numerous patents. What the West considers an achievement reserved for the most brilliant tech geniuses is merely an "entry ticket" here. They are also the most hardworking people I've ever met. We meet anytime, on weekends, in different cities. One founder even came to see us on the day his wife gave birth! However, independent thinking, a rebellious spirit, and the vision to go from zero to one are hard to find. The founders share highly similar backgrounds, their business plans tend to be risk-averse, and their ideas are often "impressive V2 versions" of existing things rather than truly original gambles. Given the scale of China's tech talent pool, I had expected to see more unheard-of, innovative ideas. My view is that China's education system produces excellence, but it doesn't leave enough room for "deviation." As a result, these founders are brilliant executors of known problems, not those who can discover "problems no one knew existed." VCs are reinforcing this model. Even more interestingly, local investors are actively amplifying this model. Most Chinese funds' investment logic revolves entirely around supporting elite alumni of companies like ByteDance or DJI—valuing background over quirks, seniority over conviction. The VCs' own backgrounds reflect this: most come from large companies, consulting firms, or investment banks, similar to European VCs a decade ago. Ironically, historically, China's greatest founders—those who truly built epoch-making companies—never worked for large corporations. Jack Ma was an English teacher who failed the college entrance exam twice; Ren Zhengfei founded Huawei after leaving the military at 43; Liu Qiangdong started his business from a street stall in Zhongguancun; Dr. Wang Xing dropped out of school and started his entrepreneurial journey from day one. A recent example is Liang Wenfeng, who had never worked outside his own company before founding DeepSeek. These are all "outliers," people lacking so-called qualifications—precisely the kind of profile the current system misses. There is real alpha in the search for this kind of talent, but currently, it seems few are paying attention. Shenzhen and the Hardware Ecosystem What struck me most in China wasn't startup pitches. Instead, it's Shenzhen's underground hardware world—in its workshops, engineers systematically acquire high-end Western products, disassemble components one by one, and reverse engineer them with extreme precision. As I left, I wasn't sure if most Western hardware founders understood what kind of competitors they were facing. The network effects here aren't theoretical; they're physical, intensive, and decades in the making. The data from the businesses we encountered corroborated this: over 70% of hardware investment came from the Greater Bay Area, nearly 100% from mainland China—allowing for iteration cycles at a speed that Western hardware companies simply couldn't match. Most founders I've met follow the "DJI model": Develop consumer hardware in a niche market (like electric wheelchairs, lawnmowers, or next-generation fitness equipment), achieve eight- to nine-figure revenue, and then leverage their customer base or underlying technology to expand into adjacent categories. Some of these businesses have grown far beyond what one might imagine. The most impressive company I encountered was Topzhu, a 3D printing company largely unknown to Westerners, which reportedly generates $500 million in annual profit, doubling every year. Pessimistic View of Chinese Software When I left, my skepticism about the opportunities in Chinese software exceeded my initial skepticism. At the model level, China's open-source models are indeed impressive—but closed-source models still lag significantly behind the top Western players, and this gap is likely to widen. The capital expenditure (CapEx) gap is huge, and GPU acquisition remains limited. Western labs are increasingly restricting model distillation. Revenue figures clearly illustrate the problem: Anthropic reportedly generated $6 billion in revenue in February alone. Meanwhile, the best-performing Chinese startups only generate tens of millions of dollars in annual recurring revenue (ARR). In the software startup arena, the mainstream profile consists of former ByteDance product managers and researchers building agent or environmentally aware consumer software for Western markets. Talent certainly exists, but many products are already covered by the core features of large companies—they become redundant as soon as the large companies release a version. I am also struck by the lack of large, rapidly growing privately held software companies. In the West, besides model companies, several startups (such as Cursor, Loveable, ElevenLabs, Harvey, and Glaan) are printing nine- and ten-figure ARR at astonishing growth rates. Such breakout private software companies are virtually nonexistent in China—a few exceptions like HeyGen, Manus, and GenSpark, who ultimately chose to leave once they saw an opportunity. Valuation Bubble Despite the promising outlook for software, bubbles exist in both early and late stages. In the early stages, while top talent from ByteDance, DeepSeek, and Moonshot is still significantly cheaper than their American counterparts, median valuations have converged. It's common for consumer startups to be valued at $100-200 million before even launching a product. Pre-seed funding exceeding $30 million is also not uncommon. In the later stages, the numbers are even more inconsistent. Minimax was traded at approximately $40 billion on the public market, while its ARR was less than $100 million—approximately a price-to-sales ratio of 400. Zhipu is valued at approximately $25 billion against $50 million in revenue. For comparison, OpenAI's peak funding round had a price-to-sales ratio of approximately 66x, and Anthropic's was approximately 61x. Private companies like Moonlit Night are using these publicly traded targets as benchmarks, raising funds at valuations of $6 billion, $10 billion, and $18 billion in just a few months. Cryptocurrency investors will recognize this dynamic: investors are comparing private valuations to unreleased fair market prices. Furthermore, part of the reason supporting Zhipu and Minimax at these valuation levels is that they are currently the only way to gain exposure to the "China AI narrative," thus carrying a premium. As more companies go public and dilute this characteristic, the situation will change. Finally, the IPO window often closes very quickly and without warning—you can't be sure you'll be able to profit from this arbitrage before the benchmark price changes. The same applies to the humanoid robotics sector. There are approximately 200 humanoid robotics companies in China, about 20 of which have raised over $100 million, and several have reached valuations of billions of dollars—almost all without revenue, and most plan to IPO in Hong Kong in 2026 or 2027. If this market is real, China's hardware dominance makes the long-term outcome quite clear. However, commercialization may be slower than the current pace of funding suggests, and I doubt the Hong Kong market can support so many humanoid robotics companies currently planning to raise billions of dollars. I'm currently choosing to wait and see.

A Notable Asymmetry

One thing I didn't expect: almost every founder I met was building products for the global market, not just the Chinese market. They used Claude Code and watched Dwarkesh's videos. They knew the San Francisco startup landscape inside and out, often better than Western investors who didn't pay much attention to the details.

Western hostility towards China is far deeper than Chinese hostility towards the West. Chinese founders believe that combining Chinese engineering execution and hardware depth with Western go-to-market (GTM) strategies and product vision is not contradictory. This combination, when found in the right founding teams, can produce some truly exceptional companies.

Our current focus is on identifying these founders—outliers who don't fit the "elite qualifications" template optimized for the local VC ecosystem.

data.title: Hollywood's AI-powered necromancy: Death is no longer the end of labor.
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

In 2025, Val Kilmer passed away from complications of throat cancer at the age of 65. The Iceman, once the spirited hero in *Top Gun*, and the cool and charismatic Bruce Wayne in *Batman Forever*, suffered greatly from cancer in his later years.

He was a devout Christian Scientologist who rejected modern medicine, attempting to cure his illness through prayer. This ultimately cost him his voice and his life.

However, less than a year after his death, he was "resurrected" in a film about Native American spirituality, *Deep as a Grave*. This was the first time in film history that generative AI technology was used to allow a deceased actor to give a completely new performance.

A soul who most rejected modern technology in life, even trying to use theology to fight against pathology, was transformed into a digital specimen by the most advanced modern technology after death.

We once thought death was the only fair thing. But now it seems that the poor are forgotten after death, while the rich continue to work for capitalists. The story of *Deep as a Grave* takes place in DeCelli Canyon, Arizona, a sacred site for the Navajo people. Val Kilmer plays a Catholic priest and Native American spiritualist who follows two archaeologists on an excavation in the canyon, attempting to find the final resting place of ancient souls. The film's underlying tone is one of reverence, a questioning of the vanished civilization on this red land. But reality is particularly ironic. In Navajo traditional culture, death is an extremely dangerous taboo. They believe that after death, a malevolent aura called "Chindi" remains, which leaves the body with the last breath, taking away all imbalance and evil thoughts. The Navajo people deeply revere death. They avoid talking about the dead, never call the deceased by name, and are extremely averse to touching the deceased's belongings. In their minds, forcibly disturbing the peace of the dead will bring great calamity. The film *Deep as a Grave*, which claims to "respect Indigenous history," uses the most offensive method to the dead: AI forcibly brings Val Kilmer back to life. To complete the scenes he couldn't finish due to illness, Silicon Valley engineers collected video footage, audio clips, and even his hoarse breathing in the late stages of throat cancer, feeding these digital remains into an algorithm. Ultimately, in a cold server room, they calculated the priest in the film discussing the soul's destiny in the canyon. Does Hollywood not know this is an offense to Navajo culture? Of course they do. But they don't care; they care more about financial reports and valuations. How much money can a deceased actor actually make for living capitalists? To answer this question, we need to understand a newly emerging business model in Hollywood. According to Forbes' "Highest-Paid Dead Celebrities" list, superstars like Michael Jackson continue to generate hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue annually after their deaths. In the past, this "post-death economics" relied on copyright licensing, such as selling tapes, merchandise, and tribute concerts. Estate companies simply collected rent, profiting from the assets accumulated by the stars during their lifetimes. But the emergence of AI has completely changed this business model. According to an in-depth analysis by Hollywood industry media outlet *The Ankler*, California recently expanded its posthumous image rights law, explicitly including AI-generated digital stand-ins. This means that estate companies are no longer selling "past works," but rather the "working time" of celebrities after their deaths. The commercialization of posthumous IP has officially transitioned from copyright licensing to an era of productivity extraction. For film studios, this is a perfect business loop. In traditional film production, actors are the most uncontrollable variable. They age, gain weight, argue with the crew over pay, and have their films pulled from theaters due to scandals in their private lives. They might even unite to form unions and launch a massive strike that lasts for six months. But AI-resurrected actors won't. Capitalists have finally found the perfect employees. Digital Kilmer will never grow old. He doesn't need a motorhome, he doesn't need rest, he has no temper, he won't join a union, and he'll always be obedient. Ask him to play a priest, and he'll play a priest; ask him to recite a sad line, and his algorithmically calculated digital face will squeeze out the most precise tear. Marx predicted in *Capital* that capital would squeeze every drop of blood and sweat from the workers, but he probably never imagined that in Hollywood in 2026, even the residual value of the dead could be squeezed dry. Who is selling out Kilmer? In this digital resurrection, Kilmer's daughter plays a key role. Facing external controversy, she publicly stated her full support for the production company's use of AI to resurrect her father. Her reasoning was: "My father was a deeply spiritual person; he always viewed emerging technologies with optimism, seeing them as tools to expand the possibilities of art." Indeed, Val Kilmer, in order to have a dignified farewell to his old friend in *Top Gun: Maverick*, had to compromise and allow AI technology to recreate his lost voice. His daughter used this as grounds to claim that her father was optimistic about technology. This effectively cloaked the production company in a legal and ethical guise. However, the families and capitalists have misinterpreted the concept. A living person who voluntarily uses a digital prosthetic to complete their artistic swan song is not necessarily willing to have their soul and body completely separated after death, becoming a puppet to be manipulated. Compromise in life is to defend dignity; resurrection after death is utter deprivation. In 2023, the Screen Actors Guild in the United States launched a 118-day strike to resist AI replacement. The final agreement stipulated that the resurrection of deceased actors by AI must be explicitly authorized by the estate (usually the family) and that appropriate compensation must be paid. The union thought its strike had built a strong fortress, but reality proved it merely left a backdoor for capital. Now, capital doesn't need to defeat the union; it only needs to overwhelm the families with money. Val Kilmer may have been optimistic about technology during his lifetime, but that doesn't mean he was willing to entrust his face and voice to a character he never read the script for or participated in for even a second of filming after his death. In an era without digital wills, the dead become the most silent lambs to the slaughter. Capitalists and their families have divided the spoils, but will the audience, as the ones paying the bill, really see the "performance" they want to see on screen? The Uncanny Valley of Electronic Pre-made Dishes As it turns out, the audience simply didn't want to see it. A Wired magazine in-depth report points out that today's audiences have developed a strong aversion to AI-generated entertainment content. No matter how much studios tout technological breakthroughs, all viewers see are dead fish eyes, distorted micro-expressions, and a creepy plastic feel. This aversion isn't due to moral fastidiousness, but rather to the uncanny valley effect—a physiological instinct in humans. When a non-human object is very similar to a human in appearance and movement, but is not entirely human, it evokes strong aversion and disgust in the viewer. German philosopher Walter Benjamin, in *The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction*, proposed a famous concept: "Aura." He believed that true works of art possess a uniqueness specific to the here and now; this unrepeatable sense of presence is "Aura." The AI-generated Val Kilmer has been completely drained of his spirit. He lacks the weight of a physical body, the sluggishness of breathing, and the unpredictability of improvisation. Every expression he makes is an algorithmic calculation of the average of past data. Val Kilmer's resurrection is not a technological miracle, but rather a pre-made electronic meal forced upon audiences by Hollywood independent studios facing budget constraints. If AI has drained the spirit of acting, then what is true, moving acting? The Iceman's Tears, the Fragmented Reality To answer this question, we only need to turn back the clock four years. In 2022, Val Kilmer played the Iceman in the film *Top Gun: Maverick*. At that time, he had already undergone a tracheotomy due to throat cancer, completely lost his voice, was emaciated, and his body was extremely frail. The director did not use CGI to restore him to his youthful appearance, nor did he conceal his illness. In the film, the Iceman also suffers from throat cancer and can only communicate with Tom Cruise by typing on a computer keyboard. In that scene, Iceman typed a line: "It's time to let go." Tom Cruise looked at the screen, his eyes reddening, and tears welled up in his eyes. Then, Iceman struggled to let out a hoarse, extremely weak sigh. At that moment, all the viewers were moved. Because it was real flesh enduring pain; it was two old friends, entangled for thirty years, giving a dignified farewell with their broken bodies. That incomplete beauty tinged with the shadow of death, that vulnerability and dignity displayed by humanity in the face of disease, is something no top-of-the-line graphics card could render. And in 2026's *Deep as a Grave*, AI rejuvenated Val Kilmer's appearance, giving him a perfect voice. He no longer suffered, no longer needed tubes; he achieved immortality in the digital world. In the real world, a decaying flesh exists; in the digital world, a perpetually gleaming surrogate remains. Do we truly love the one suffering in reality, or the perfect digital reflection? When viewers shed tears over a piece of code-generated micro-expression of sadness, what are we moved by? Ultimately, we can only empathize with real suffering, not with a string of perfect data. Real imperfection is always more powerful than false perfection. A Labor Contract Without End Val Kilmer suffered greatly from illness before his death. He lost his voice because he refused medical treatment and could only be fed through a tube due to a tracheotomy. In the last years of his life, his body became a prison. He should have found peace in death. But in Hollywood today, death is no longer the end of labor, but the beginning of a new contract with no end in sight. His image, his voice, his lifetime of performance data are packaged into an asset package called "Val Kilmer," continuing to generate box office revenue for others on screen. In today's era of AI, when we look at those resurrected stars, we are actually looking at our future selves. When our data, habits, voices, and images can be perfectly replicated by algorithms, and even packaged and sold while we are still alive, the physical presence of the body becomes irrelevant. Technology once promised to liberate humanity from arduous labor, but in reality, it has turned humanity itself into a means of production that can be infinitely replicated. In life, it robbed you of your uniqueness; in death, it even confiscates your right to rest. The Navajo people were right. Let the dead rest in peace; do not disturb their souls. For when you gaze into the abyss, you see not only the ghosts of the departed, but also the greedy eyes of capitalists.
